label: PROBATE
title: Beneficiary Interpleader Litigation
description: When competing claims arise over life insurance proceeds or estate assets, we represent clients in interpleader actions to determine the rightful recipient.

sections.heading: Life Insurance Attorney, Houston Texas
sections.body: When competing beneficiary claims arise over life insurance proceeds or other assets, an interpleader action allows a court to determine the rightful recipient. The firm represents both claimants and stakeholders in these disputes throughout Harris County and the greater Houston area.

sections.heading: Contested Wills & Fiduciary Litigation
sections.body: The firm handles will contest cases and fiduciary litigation matters including executor mismanagement, trustee embezzlement, failure to provide financial disclosure, and conflicts of interest. Clients receive clear, strategic guidance from an attorney with over two decades of probate experience.

sections.heading: Contact a Houston Interpleader Attorney
sections.body: If you are involved in a dispute over life insurance benefits, estate assets, or a fiduciary breach, contact the Law Office of Troy M. Moore for an honest assessment and committed representation.

label: PERSONAL INJURY
title: Car Accidents
description: Injured in a car accident? We help victims recover compensation for medical bills, lost wages, and pain and suffering caused by negligent drivers.

sections.heading: Local Car Wreck Accident Attorney
sections.body: The aftermath of a car accident can be difficult, with recovery complicated by lost work time, medical bills, and vehicle repair costs. Texas law requires drivers to remain at accident scenes and holds negligent drivers liable for the harm they cause to others on the road.

sections.heading: Motor Vehicle Accidents in Texas
sections.body: Negligent driving — including distracted driving, intoxication, and poor vehicle maintenance — causes preventable accidents every day. Texas law protects victims by allowing them to seek compensation for personal injuries sustained from another's negligent conduct.

sections.heading: Types of Injuries
sections.body: Impact forces in motor vehicle accidents cause varied serious injuries including spinal cord damage, fractures, whiplash, internal injuries, and head trauma. Some injuries appear only later, and victims may suffer psychological effects requiring months or years of costly medical care.

sections.heading: Importance of Legal Representation
sections.body: Skilled car accident attorneys ensure cases receive proper attention and that justice is served. Texas law limits the time victims have to pursue damages, making prompt contact with experienced counsel essential to protecting your rights and maximizing your recovery.

label: PERSONAL INJURY
title: Distracted Driving Accidents
description: Texting while driving is more dangerous than drunk driving. We aggressively pursue full compensation for victims of distracted driving crashes throughout Houston.

sections.heading: A Serious & Growing Danger
sections.body: Research demonstrates that texting while driving impairs judgment more severely than alcohol consumption. Studies from Car and Driver and the Transport Research Laboratory showed that texters had slower response times and worse lane control than intoxicated drivers, making distracted driving one of the most dangerous behaviors on Texas roads.

sections.heading: Comprehensive Legal Service
sections.body: The firm handles medical bill claims, insurance negotiations with multiple carriers, property damage replacement, opposing counsel negotiations, and litigation when necessary. Attorneys also assist with psychological trauma recovery and work closely with clients through every development in their case.

sections.heading: Why You Need an Attorney
sections.body: Insurance companies routinely attempt to minimize payouts after distracted driving crashes. An experienced attorney investigates phone records, witness statements, and accident reconstruction evidence to build a compelling case and hold negligent drivers fully accountable for the harm they caused.

label: PERSONAL INJURY
title: Dog Bite Lawyer
description: Dog bite victims deserve compensation under Texas law. We handle cases where homeowner's insurance covers claims from injuries inflicted by dangerous dogs — no fee unless we win.

sections.heading: Dangerous Dogs Pose a Threat
sections.body: Once a vicious animal bites another person, this legally notifies the owner of the dog's dangerous nature. Subsequent bites make the owner civilly responsible in a court of law for the victim's medical bills, pain and suffering, disfigurement, and lost wages.

sections.heading: Leash Laws & Owner Liability
sections.body: Leash laws require dog owners to restrain their animals in public spaces. Violations of these laws support negligence liability arguments and demonstrate that the owner failed their duty to protect the community from unpredictable dog behavior.

sections.heading: The "One Bite" Rule
sections.body: Texas law effectively grants dogs one free bite before owners face strict liability. Once a dog has bitten someone, the owner must answer in court for any future bites — and an experienced attorney can help establish that prior notice to maximize your compensation.

label: PROBATE
title: Flat Fee Probate Services
description: Straightforward, transparent pricing for Texas probate — whether a decedent had a will or died intestate. No billing surprises during an already difficult time.

sections.heading: Probate with a Will
sections.body: Two main options for estates with a will: Probate of a Will with Independent Administration ($4,550) for cases requiring full administration with executor appointment, and Muniment of Title ($3,950) for simpler situations with no unpaid debts, typically filed more than four years after death.

sections.heading: Probate Without a Will (Intestate)
sections.body: Four options are available for intestate estates: Small Estate Affidavit ($3,450) for estates under $75,000; Judicial Determination of Heirship ($7,150) when heirs need legal identification; Judicial Determination with Administration ($8,150) for complex estates; and Affidavit of Heirship ($3,250) as the lowest-cost option when all heirs agree.

sections.heading: Why Choose Flat Fee Probate
sections.body: The firm emphasizes flat fee transparency with known costs upfront, local Harris County expertise, efficient step-by-step guidance, and compassionate support during difficult times. Families can focus on healing while experienced attorneys handle every aspect of the probate process.

label: PROBATE
title: Contested Will & Fiduciary Litigation
description: Disputes over wills, trusts, and fiduciary duties require skilled advocacy. We protect your inheritance rights with over 25 years of Texas probate litigation experience.

sections.heading: Grounds for Contesting a Will in Texas
sections.body: Texas law allows interested parties to challenge a will on several grounds: undue influence — a caregiver or family member overcame the testator's free will to benefit themselves; lack of testamentary capacity — the testator did not understand what they were signing, what they owned, or who their natural heirs were; forgery or fraud in the will's execution; and failure to meet the formal legal requirements of the Texas Estates Code. Each ground requires specific evidence and a focused legal strategy built on decades of probate court experience.

sections.heading: Fiduciary Litigation
sections.body: Fiduciary obligations exist whenever someone is entrusted to manage assets on behalf of another — including executors of estates, trustees of trusts, and guardians of incapacitated adults. When fiduciaries breach those duties through self-dealing, embezzlement, failure to provide financial accounting to beneficiaries, investment mismanagement, or conflicts of interest, affected parties have legal recourse. The firm litigates fiduciary breach claims throughout Harris County, Fort Bend County, and Montgomery County.

sections.heading: Time Is Critical — Act Now
sections.body: Texas probate law imposes strict deadlines for filing objections and contesting probate proceedings. If you believe an executor is mismanaging an estate, a trustee is breaching their fiduciary duties, or a will does not reflect the decedent's true wishes, contact Troy M. Moore immediately. Waiting can forfeit your rights entirely — and the earlier an attorney is involved, the stronger your position.

sections.heading: Speak With a Houston Will Contest Lawyer
sections.body: The Law Office of Troy M. Moore, PLLC represents surviving spouses, adult children, named beneficiaries, and other interested parties in will contest and fiduciary breach cases throughout the greater Houston area. You deserve an honest assessment of your case and aggressive representation committed to protecting what your loved one intended and what you are rightfully owed. Contact the firm today for a consultation.

label: ESTATE PLANNING
title: Transfer on Death Deed (TODD)
description: A TODD deed lets you name a beneficiary to inherit real estate upon your death while bypassing probate entirely — retaining full control during your lifetime.

sections.heading: What Is a TODD Deed?
sections.body: A Transfer on Death Deed enables property owners to designate beneficiaries to inherit real estate without probate proceedings. In Texas, TODD deeds are governed by Chapter 114 of the Texas Estates Code and must be carefully prepared to ensure their validity and enforceability at the time of the owner's death.

sections.heading: Advantages
sections.body: Key benefits include avoiding probate entirely, retaining full ownership and control during your lifetime, cost-effectiveness compared to trust-based estate planning, and flexibility to revoke or modify the deed at any time before death.

sections.heading: Important Limitations
sections.body: TODD deeds apply to real estate only and cannot serve as a complete substitute for comprehensive estate planning. Potential creditor claims, title transfer disputes from improperly executed documents, and other complexities mean professional legal preparation is essential.

label: ESTATE PLANNING
title: Last Will & Testament
description: Without a valid will, Texas courts decide who inherits your property. We help you protect your family with a carefully drafted will tailored to your circumstances.

sections.heading: Why Do I Need a Will?
sections.body: At the time of your death, your estate goes through a probate process. A will ensures your assets are distributed according to your wishes rather than letting courts decide. Without one, your estate becomes intestate and is vulnerable to litigation and disputes from potential heirs who may contest the distribution.

sections.heading: Keep Your Will Up-to-Date
sections.body: Circumstances like divorce, the death of a beneficiary, business succession events, or significant increases in assets make updating your will essential. An outdated will is more prone to probate litigation and can create problems nearly as serious as having no will at all.

sections.heading: Key Factors When Drafting a Will
sections.body: Important considerations include naming an executor, cataloging assets like homes and retirement accounts, listing beneficiaries clearly, and appointing guardians for minor children. Professional guidance ensures all crucial details are properly included and legally enforceable under Texas law.

label: ESTATE PLANNING
title: Living Wills & Advance Directives
description: A living will (Directive to Physicians) expresses your medical treatment wishes if you become incapacitated — a critical component of any complete estate plan.

sections.heading: What Is an Advance Directive?
sections.body: Advance directives are legal documents — such as a living will or a power of attorney — in which a person expresses their desires for medical treatment in case of disability. These documents outline instructions for care and end-of-life decision-making, becoming active once you have been diagnosed with a terminal or irreversible condition.

sections.heading: Terminal vs. Irreversible Condition
sections.body: A terminal condition will likely end your life within six months in reasonable medical probability, while an irreversible condition will eventually be fatal but not necessarily within that timeframe. Living wills apply only when a physician has diagnosed you with one of these specific conditions.

sections.heading: Living Will vs. Medical Power of Attorney
sections.body: A living will communicates your personal treatment preferences directly to physicians, whereas a medical power of attorney designates another person to make medical decisions on your behalf when you cannot do so yourself. Both are distinct from — and complementary to — a Last Will & Testament.

label: PERSONAL INJURY
title: Motorcycle Accident Attorney
description: Insurance companies often undervalue motorcycle injury claims and shift blame onto the rider. We fight to ensure injured riders receive the full compensation they deserve.

sections.heading: Protecting Injured Riders
sections.body: Insurance companies often try to settle cases under value and may attempt to shift blame onto the motorcycle rider. The firm provides vigorous legal representation for those suffering from motorcycle accidents in Cypress, Tomball, Northwest Houston, and throughout Texas.

sections.heading: Common Motorcycle Injuries
sections.body: Serious crashes cause varied and severe injuries including road rash, traumatic brain injuries, fractures, spinal cord damage, internal bleeding, paralysis, and limb loss. These injuries result in chronic pain, high medical expenses, significant lost wages, and often require extensive ongoing treatment and rehabilitation.

sections.heading: After a Motorcycle Crash
sections.body: Seek immediate medical attention, gather insurance information and witness contact details at the scene, and follow all doctor recommendations. Contact an attorney before speaking with any insurance adjusters — the firm operates on contingency, meaning no fee unless we win.

label: ESTATE PLANNING
title: Probate Avoidance Package™
description: A comprehensive estate planning bundle designed to help your family bypass probate court entirely — protecting assets, privacy, and family wealth while saving thousands.

sections.heading: Why Avoid Probate?
sections.body: Everything is more expensive today, including probate court and associated legal fees. A Last Will & Testament alone requires court involvement and is just a list of instructions that must be executed through an administrative lawsuit — adding significant cost, delay, and public exposure for grieving families.

sections.heading: What the Package Includes
sections.body: Seven components: a basic Last Will & Testament, Transfer on Death Deed, Medical Power of Attorney, Directive to Physicians, Financial Power of Attorney, vehicle beneficiary designations, and a 30-minute attorney consultation on financial account setup.

sections.heading: Estimated Savings
sections.body: The firm estimates potential savings of $5,000 or more per person through careful probate avoidance planning. Strategic asset positioning enables families to sidestep probate entirely — keeping estates private and preventing creditor exposure in the process.

label: ESTATE PLANNING
title: Self-Directed IRA Attorney
description: Self-directed IRAs allow alternative investments like real estate while maintaining tax-advantaged status. An attorney helps ensure IRS compliance and protects your retirement assets.

sections.heading: What Is a Self-Directed IRA?
sections.body: Self-directed IRAs grant investors control over alternative investments like real estate, tax liens, private businesses, mortgages, and secured notes. Unlike standard retirement accounts, the account owner makes all investment decisions and must maintain compliance with IRS requirements.

sections.heading: Benefits
sections.body: Account holders enjoy complete control over investment choices, potential for higher returns than traditional markets, portfolio diversification beyond volatile public markets, and the ability to invest in tangible assets like real estate with limited downside risk compared to equities.

sections.heading: Our Legal Services
sections.body: Comprehensive services include determining appropriate retirement plan structures, establishing LLC entities for IRA investments, drafting contracts, obtaining financing, preparing tax filings, and providing ongoing advisory support for maintaining compliance with IRS and Department of Labor regulations.

label: PROBATE
title: Small Estate Affidavit
description: For estates under $75,000 when there is no will, a Small Estate Affidavit offers a streamlined alternative to full probate — saving time, cost, and court complexity.

sections.heading: What Is a Small Estate Affidavit in Texas?
sections.body: When a decedent dies without a will and the value of the estate property is worth less than $75,000 (not counting the value of the house), a Small Estate Affidavit offers an alternative to lengthy probate proceedings. The affidavit transfers the deceased person's property to rightful heirs and includes signatures of two disinterested witnesses, all heirs, relationship details, and family structure information.

sections.heading: How the Process Works
sections.body: All heirs must sign the affidavit before a notary public. The office then files the documents with the county clerk, where a probate court judge reviews and signs the form. Once approved, certified copies are obtained for the client's records, completing the transfer of assets to rightful heirs.

sections.heading: Cost & Filing
sections.body: Filing fees apply and vary by county. As part of our flat-fee service, we determine the specific fee from the clerk's office and handle the payment — ensuring no surprise costs for grieving families.

label: ESTATE PLANNING
title: Special Needs Trust
description: Set aside funds for a loved one with special needs while protecting their eligibility for Medicaid, SSI, and other essential government assistance programs.

sections.heading: What Is a Special Needs Trust?
sections.body: A special needs trust — also called a supplemental needs trust — enables families to provide ongoing financial support for disabled individuals without disqualifying them from government benefits. These trusts disburse funds gradually to cover living expenses, protecting beneficiaries from asset limits imposed by programs like Medicaid and SSI.

sections.heading: Protecting Government Benefit Eligibility
sections.body: Without proper planning, an inheritance or large gift can disqualify a disabled individual from Medicaid, SSI, and other essential benefit programs. A properly drafted special needs trust holds and distributes assets in a way that supplements — rather than replaces — government assistance.

sections.heading: Tailored to Your Family
sections.body: Attorney Troy Moore guides clients through establishing special needs trusts tailored to their family's unique circumstances, ensuring disabled individuals are not left with unmanageable lump sums and exploring additional care arrangements like guardianship to comprehensively protect vulnerable loved ones.

label: PROBATE
title: Texas Probate Attorney
description: Expert probate services for Northwest Houston, Cypress, Spring, and Tomball — from will administration through heirship determination and full estate distribution.

sections.heading: Probate Administration of a Will
sections.body: Probate of a will involves filing an application, attending court hearings to validate the will, obtaining Letters Testamentary, publishing creditor notices, notifying beneficiaries, and recording documents with county offices where real property is held. The firm guides executors and beneficiaries through each step.

sections.heading: Muniment of Title
sections.body: This Texas-specific option allows probate to proceed outside the standard four-year filing window. When the statute of limitations has expired, a will can still be probated by notifying all heirs with special statutory notice or obtaining heir consent.

sections.heading: Heirship & Intestacy
sections.body: For those who died without a will, heirship proceedings determine who inherits and in what percentages through Texas intestacy law. The process includes filing applications, appointing attorneys ad litem, presenting witness testimony, and typically includes estate administration to distribute the deceased's property to rightful heirs.

label: PROBATE
title: Inheritance & Intestacy Attorney
description: When someone dies without a will in Texas, state intestacy laws determine who inherits — which may not reflect the deceased's wishes. We ensure assets reach their rightful heirs.

sections.heading: Navigating Texas Intestacy Law
sections.body: When someone passes away without a will in Texas, their estate follows the state's intestacy laws, which may not reflect the deceased's actual wishes. Our attorneys investigate family circumstances and dynamics to ensure proper asset distribution through descent and distribution proceedings under the Texas Estates Code.

sections.heading: Who Inherits Under Texas Intestacy?
sections.body: Texas intestacy law addresses a surviving spouse's community property rights, children's inheritance (they inherit everything absent a surviving spouse), and other relatives' claims — including parents, siblings, nieces, and nephews — when no spouse or children survive the decedent.

sections.heading: Experienced, Compassionate Representation
sections.body: The firm offers decades of experience managing probate and heirship cases, provides personalized and compassionate representation, maintains clear communication throughout all proceedings, and delivers cost-effective solutions through efficient resolutions that minimize the burden on grieving families.

label: PERSONAL INJURY
title: Truck Accident Attorney
description: Catastrophic collisions with 18-wheelers and commercial trucks cause life-altering injuries. We hold negligent trucking companies and drivers fully accountable.

sections.heading: Types of Truck Accidents We Handle
sections.body: The firm has decades of experience representing families following semi-truck collisions, jackknife accidents, rollover incidents, brake failures, underride crashes, and numerous other commercial vehicle accident scenarios involving 18-wheelers and large trucks throughout Houston and Texas.

sections.heading: Common Causes & Liability
sections.body: The majority of truck accidents involve driver error including fatigue, distracted driving, improperly loaded cargo, inadequate vehicle maintenance, and speeding. Trucking companies must comply with strict Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration standards, and violations of those rules can establish liability against the company.

sections.heading: After a Truck Accident
sections.body: After a truck crash, contact emergency services immediately, gather witness information, document the scene with photographs, and obtain insurance details. An experienced attorney will investigate driver logbooks, black box data, and maintenance records to assess liability and pursue full compensation on your behalf.

label: ESTATE PLANNING
title: Houston Trusts Attorney
description: Living trusts help avoid probate and provide seamless, private asset distribution to your beneficiaries. We establish and protect trusts tailored to your long-term goals.

sections.heading: What Is a Living Trust?
sections.body: A living trust is a powerful estate planning instrument that ensures asset protection and allows beneficiaries to bypass probate court proceedings entirely. Unlike a will alone, a living trust provides a private, efficient mechanism for transferring wealth across generations without the delays, costs, and public exposure of the probate process. Trusts can be either revocable — changeable during your lifetime — or irrevocable, offering different levels of asset protection and tax treatment.

sections.heading: Types of Trusts We Create
sections.body: The Law Office of Troy M. Moore, PLLC drafts and administers a full range of trust instruments, including revocable living trusts, marital trusts, grandparents' trusts, Miller trusts, testamentary trusts, special needs trusts, bypass trusts, and charitable trusts. Each trust is drafted to reflect your specific family circumstances, asset profile, and long-term goals — not pulled from a generic template.

sections.heading: Benefits of a Living Trust
sections.body: Trusts offer advantages that wills alone cannot provide: they avoid the costly and time-consuming probate process entirely, reduce administrative expenses for your family, may provide estate tax advantages, maintain complete privacy regarding your assets and beneficiaries, and remain fully revocable during the grantor's lifetime — allowing you to make changes as your circumstances change.

sections.heading: Trust Disputes & Litigation
sections.body: The firm represents trustees, settlors, and beneficiaries in trust-related disputes, including interpretation of trust terms, funding disputes, mental capacity challenges, undue influence allegations, modification and termination issues, distribution disagreements, successor trustee disputes, fraud allegations, and breach of fiduciary duty claims. With over 25 years of probate and trust litigation experience, Troy M. Moore provides skilled advocacy when disputes arise.

label: PROBATE
title: Life Insurance Attorney
description: If a life insurance company has denied your claim, delayed payment, or filed an interpleader lawsuit, Troy M. Moore fights to recover the benefits you are lawfully owed.

panel.headline: Life Insurance Attorney in Houston, TX

sections.heading: Life Insurance Claims & Wrongful Denials
sections.body: Are you frustrated dealing with the life insurance company over the death of a loved one, and they will not listen to you or pay you? Are you a beneficiary whose claim has been wrongfully denied? This is a serious area of the law that requires specific experience — not your best friend's older brother who happens to be a lawyer. The Law Office of Troy M. Moore, PLLC has been successfully pursuing and prosecuting interpleader and life insurance litigation cases for over 25 years throughout Harris County and the greater Houston area.

sections.heading: What Is an Interpleader?
sections.body: When a life insurance company has more than one person claiming entitlement to the same policy benefits, the insurer may file a lawsuit called an interpleader. The life insurance company deposits the policy proceeds into the court registry (the court's bank account) and lets the rival claimants fight in court over who has the superior right to the money. An experienced interpleader attorney will represent your claim aggressively and ensure the court hears your side of the dispute.

sections.heading: Types of Policies We Handle
sections.body: The firm handles disputes involving all types of life insurance policies: state life insurance policies (all U.S. states), Universal Life (UL) policies, ERISA life insurance policies, term life insurance policies, whole-life insurance policies, variable life insurance policies, and annuity contracts. Whether your claim was denied, delayed, or disputed by a competing beneficiary, we evaluate your situation and advise you on your strongest legal options.

sections.heading: Speak With a Houston Life Insurance Attorney
sections.body: If a life insurance company is failing to pay you what is rightfully yours — or another family member is causing trouble by disputing the claim — contact the Law Office of Troy M. Moore, PLLC immediately. Do not sign anything until you have spoken with an attorney. The firm will tirelessly work to recover the compensation you deserve under Texas law and ensure that your legitimate claim is resolved in your favor.

label: ESTATE PLANNING
title: Financial Power of Attorney
description: A Statutory Durable Power of Attorney ensures someone you trust can manage your financial affairs if you become incapacitated — avoiding the expense and delay of court-ordered guardianship.

panel.headline: Houston Financial Power of Attorney Lawyer

sections.heading: What Is a Statutory Durable Power of Attorney?
sections.body: A statutory durable power of attorney is a legal document that allows you to designate someone — your "agent" — to make financial and business decisions on your behalf if you become incapacitated. The word "durable" means the document remains in effect even after incapacitation. These are especially important for managing personal finances, real property, and business interests when illness or injury prevents you from acting for yourself.

sections.heading: What Your Agent Can Do
sections.body: The person you designate can typically access your funds to handle daily concerns such as paying bills, mortgages, and taxes; monitor and manage accounts; make investment decisions; and take over shareholder responsibilities if authorized. You control the scope — you can place specific restrictions on what your agent is and is not permitted to do, allowing whatever access matches your comfort level.

sections.heading: Why You Need an Attorney to Draft It
sections.body: A financial power of attorney that is not drafted correctly can be rejected by banks and financial institutions when your agent tries to use it — precisely when your family needs it most. Do not rely on a home-made or generic form. Troy M. Moore, PLLC drafts Statutory Durable Powers of Attorney that comply with Texas law and that financial institutions will honor. This is particularly important for same-sex couples, as Texas may not extend default legal authority to unmarried partners.

sections.heading: The Alternative: Court-Ordered Guardianship
sections.body: Without a valid financial power of attorney in place, your family must pursue a court-ordered guardianship to manage your financial affairs. Guardianship proceedings in Texas are time-consuming, expensive, and require ongoing court supervision — an added burden during an already stressful time. Establishing a Statutory Durable Power of Attorney ahead of time eliminates this problem entirely and ensures your finances are handled by someone you chose, on your terms.

label: ESTATE PLANNING
title: Medical Power of Attorney
description: A Medical Power of Attorney designates a trusted person to make healthcare decisions for you when you cannot — a critical document for every Texan's estate plan.

panel.headline: Houston Medical Power of Attorney Lawyer

sections.heading: What Is a Medical Power of Attorney?
sections.body: A Medical Power of Attorney is an estate planning document that gives authority to a trusted person — your "agent" — to make healthcare decisions on your behalf when you are unable to communicate or make decisions yourself. This advanced medical directive allows you to formally name a loved one who will act for you, ensuring that your wishes for medical treatment are honored regardless of the circumstances you face. The document only activates when you are unable to provide your own informed consent — it gives your agent no control while you are able to communicate.

sections.heading: When Is a Medical Power of Attorney Used?
sections.body: A Medical Power of Attorney is used when you cannot make healthcare decisions for yourself — for example, if you are unconscious after an accident and need emergency treatment, if you develop Alzheimer's Disease and require ongoing medical decisions, or during any serious medical event where you cannot communicate. Without this document, medical providers must rely on state default rules to determine who can speak for you, which may not reflect your preferences or appoint the person you would have chosen.

sections.heading: What Healthcare Decisions Can Your Agent Make?
sections.body: Your Medical Power of Attorney representative can make any decision related to your healthcare that you authorize, including giving, withholding, or withdrawing consent for surgical and medical treatments; decisions regarding hospitalization, nursing home placement, home health care, and nursing care; organ donation; and access to medical records. Your agent's authority can be as broad or as narrow as you specify in writing — you may restrict certain types of decisions while leaving others entirely to their judgment.

sections.heading: How It Differs from a Living Will
sections.body: A Living Will (or Directive to Physicians) is your direct declaration of treatment preferences — for instance, that you do not want life-sustaining machines if there is no hope of recovery. A Medical Power of Attorney goes further by designating a person to handle unpredictable medical situations that no advance declaration can fully anticipate. Both documents work together as part of a complete estate plan, and Troy M. Moore, PLLC drafts them in concert to ensure your healthcare wishes are fully protected in every scenario.

sections.heading: Requirements for a Valid Medical Power of Attorney in Texas
sections.body: To be legally valid under Texas law, a Medical Power of Attorney must be in writing, signed and dated by the principal, signed in the presence of at least two witnesses who are 18 or older, and acknowledged by a notary public. The document must include specific statutory language stating it becomes effective only when you are unable to provide, revoke, or withhold informed consent to your own medical care. The Law Office of Troy M. Moore, PLLC handles all execution formalities to ensure your document will be honored when it matters most — not rejected at a hospital because of a technical defect.
